title: Sepsis — Recognition & Management
oneLiner: Sepsis = life-threatening organ dysfunction from infection; needs immediate recognition (NEWS2, lactate) and time-critical ABCDE intervention — fluids, antibiotics, source control.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Septic shock (refractory hypotension)**
  - Single finding: persistent hypotension (MAP <65) despite adequate fluid bolus (≥30ml/kg crystalloid) — needs vasopressors (noradrenaline) within 1 hour, not more fluid.
- **Meningococcal sepsis / purpura fulminans**
  - Single finding: non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash with fever and hypotension — give IV ceftriaxone 2g (or cefotaxime) immediately; do not wait for LP or imaging.
- **Also consider:** massive pulmonary embolism (distended neck veins, acute right heart strain) or anaphylaxis (wheals, stridor, rapid onset) — both can mimic septic shock but demand different treatments.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** fever/shivering, confusion or reduced consciousness, tachypnoea, tachycardia, hypotension, oliguria, mottled skin, and a history of infection (chest, urine, abdomen, skin, line).
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Temperature >38°C or <36°C
  - HR >90, RR >20, SBP <100 or drop >40mmHg from baseline
  - Capillary refill >2s, peripheral vasoconstriction, altered mental state (AVPU less than 'A')
  - Local signs: crackles, suprapubic tenderness, costovertebral angle pain, peritonism, cellulitis, surgical wound erythema.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Sepsis** → infection with organ dysfunction (elevated lactate, hypotension, reduced GCS)
- **SIRS from non-infectious causes** (pancreatitis, burns, trauma, autoimmune flare) → no clear infection source, negative cultures, non-response to antibiotics
- **Anaphylaxis** → acute onset of urticaria, wheeze, stridor, hypotension after allergen exposure
- **Pulmonary embolism** → acute dyspnoea, pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, raised D-dimer, RV strain on ECG
- **Cardiogenic shock** → crackles, raised JVP, cool peripheries, sign of fluid overload
- **Hypovolaemic shock** → history of bleeding/diarrhoea, low JVP, high haematocrit
- **Adrenal crisis** → hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hyperpigmentation, poor response to fluids
- **Splitter:** Check lactate and look for a clear infection source (e.g., chest X-ray infiltrates, positive urinalysis) — if absent and rash present → consider anaphylaxis or meningitis first.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - NEWS2 (chart) – score ≥5 indicates sepsis trigger
  - Lactate (venous or arterial) – >2 mmol/L suggests tissue hypoperfusion; >4 mmol/L = high risk
  - Blood cultures (two sets) – before antibiotics if possible (but do not delay antibiotics >1 hour)
  - Urinalysis + urine culture
  - ECG (to rule out STEMI/arrhythmia if hypotensive)
  - Capillary refill, skin temperature, respiratory rate
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, creatinine, LFTs, coagulation screen, CRP, procalcitonin (if available)
  - Blood gas (ABG/VBG) – pH, lactate, glucose, base excess
  - Consider: group/crossmatch if suspected bleeding; troponin if chest pain/ECG changes
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Chest X-ray (for pneumonia) – erect or supine if unable to sit
  - **Second-line/if unclear source:** CT scan (chest/abdomen/pelvis) with IV contrast; US for biliary/renal obstruction; consider echocardiography if endocarditis or heart failure.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **qSOFA** (quick Sequential Organ Failure Assessment): ≥2 of altered mental state (GCS <15), RR ≥22, SBP ≤100 – predicts poor outcome in non-ICU setting.
  - **NEWS2** (National Early Warning Score 2): trigger for escalation at ≥5 (or ≥3 in any single parameter).
  - **Lactate clearance:** repeat lactate after 2-4 hours; failure to drop by ≥20% suggests inadequate resuscitation.
- **Never-miss test:** **Lactate** in any patient presenting with suspected infection or hypotension — omission can miss occult hypoperfusion and delay aggressive fluid resuscitation.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Follow ABCDE: give high-flow oxygen if SpO2 <92% (or <88% in CO2 retainers with COPD), secure IV access x 2 large bore.
  - **Fluid resuscitation:** IV crystalloid (e.g., Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline) – give 500ml bolus over <15 min; reassess. Repeat up to 30ml/kg in first hour if hypotensive or lactate >2. Use dynamic assessment (passive leg raise, IVC collapsibility) in frail/fluid-sensitive patients.
  - **Antibiotics:** Give broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within **1 hour** of recognition per local antimicrobial policy (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV, or ceftriaxone 2g IV + metronidazole 500mg IV; for neutropenic sepsis: piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem). *Do not delay for cultures.*
  - **Source control:** remove infected lines and drains, drain abscesses (surgical/radiology), decompress obstructed biliary/urinary tract (ERCP/JP drain).
  - **Vasopressors:** If BP remains low after 30ml/kg fluid – start noradrenaline via central line (or peripheral if not possible) titrated to MAP ≥65.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV fluids guided by dynamic assessment, lactate clearance, urine output (target >0.5ml/kg/hr), and clinical perfusion.
  - Review antibiotic spectrum at 48-72h: de-escalate based on cultures and sensitivities.
  - Monitor NEWS2 hourly. Repeat lactate at 2-4h; then 6-12h if improving.
  - Assess for occult source: line sites, pressure ulcers, sinuses, surgical wounds.
  - Involve critical care outreach / ICU early for escalating organ support (inotropes, NIV, RRT).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Ensure completion of antibiotic course (typically 7-10 days, tailored to source).
  - Arrange follow-up in sepsis clinic or GP for post-sepsis syndrome (fatigue, cognitive impairment, depression).
  - Check for residual organ failure (renal, respiratory) and arrange appropriate referrals.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: crystalloid (Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline) – e.g., 1L over 8h if stable, adjust per assessment.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC once daily (check weight, renal function).
  - Diet: NBM until source control achieved or safe swallow confirmed (if intubated or post-operative).
  - Observation frequency: NEWS2 – at least hourly for first 6 hours, then 4-hourly if stable.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (empiric broad-spectrum):**
  - *Community-acquired pneumonia:* co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS + clarithromycin 500mg IV BD (or doxycycline 200mg IV/PO if allergic)
  - *UTI / intra-abdominal:* piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV TDS (or ceftriaxone 2g IV OD + metronidazole 500mg IV TDS)
  - *Meningitis:* dexamethasone 10mg IV (before antibiotics) + ceftriaxone 2g IV BD + amoxicillin 2g IV 4-hourly (if Listeria risk)
  - *Neutropenic sepsis:* piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV QDS (or meropenem 1g IV TDS)
  - *Vasopressor:* noradrenaline 0.05-0.5 mcg/kg/min (titrate; start at 5-10 mcg/min in 50ml syringe pump)
- **Common alternatives:** Meropenem (if ESBL suspected); vancomycin (for MRSA risk) – add if line sepsis or known colonisation; gentamicin (single dose 5-7mg/kg IV) for synergistic effect – **must check levels before second dose**.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: avoid in penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis); caution in cholestatic jaundice.
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam: similar penicillin cross-reactivity; can cause neutropenia with prolonged use.
  - Ceftriaxone: avoid in neonates <41 weeks (displaces bilirubin); can precipitate gallstones.
  - Vancomycin: requires trough level monitoring (target 15-20 mg/L); give slowly over ≥1h to avoid red man syndrome.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** [check local policy] – e.g., vancomycin, gentamicin, piperacillin-tazobactam doses reduced in renal impairment; ceftriaxone mostly safe (though avoid in severe renal + hepatic failure). For meropenem, reduce frequency in CrCl <50 ml/min.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Not giving antibiotics within 1 hour** – each hour delay increases mortality. Also **giving IV vancomycin too fast** (red man syndrome) or **checking gentamicin levels before the second dose** (toxic accumulative).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Septic shock (refractory hypotension), acute kidney injury, ARDS, DIC (disseminated intravascular coagulation), metabolic acidosis, multiorgan failure, death.
- **Chronic:**
  - Post-sepsis syndrome (cognitive impairment, fatigue, PTSD), limb amputation (if purpura fulminans), chronic kidney disease, critical illness polyneuropathy.
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality from septic shock is 30-50% if not recognised early; with timely antibiotics and resuscitation can drop to 20-30%. Recovery often prolonged – months of rehabilitation for critical illness survivors.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check the source** – examine chest, urine, skin, lines, surgical wounds; take cultures before antibiotics but never delay antibiotics >1 hour.
- **Repeat lactate at 2-4 hours** – failure to drop by ≥20% means inadequate resuscitation or ongoing infection; escalate to senior for source imaging or vasopressors.
- **Document NEWS2 and sepsis care bundle** – ensures audit trail and triggers early escalation by nursing staff.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Over-resuscitating with fluids** in frail/elderly patients with known heart failure – causes pulmonary oedema. Use dynamic assessment (passive leg raise) or early vasopressors.
- **Waiting for lab results before giving antibiotics** – treat empirically; de-escalate later.
- **Assuming “warm sepsis” (wide pulse pressure, bounding pulses) is benign** – still has high lactate and organ dysfunction; needs the same urgent management.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** any new confusion, breathing difficulty, leg swelling/pain, rash, recurrence of fever, inability to tolerate oral fluids/antibiotics, or urine output <0.5ml/kg/h for >6 hours.
- **Follow-up:** GP review within 48 hours (if discharged from ED) or sepsis outpatient clinic within 1-2 weeks for post-sepsis syndrome assessment.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - MAP <65 despite 30ml/kg fluid (consider noradrenaline)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L or persistent >2 mmol/L after 2-4 hours
  - NEWS2 ≥7 (or increasing by ≥3 despite treatment)
  - GCS <13 or acute change
  - Urine output <0.5ml/kg/h for 2 consecutive hours
  - New oxygen requirement >40% FiO2 or SpO2 <92%
- **Crash team if:** PEA arrest, asystole, unresponsive, no breathing.

rawMarkdown:
## Sepsis — Recognition & Management
**One-liner:** Sepsis = life-threatening organ dysfunction from infection; needs immediate recognition (NEWS2, lactate) and time-critical ABCDE intervention — fluids, antibiotics, source control.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Septic shock (refractory hypotension)**
  - Single finding: persistent hypotension (MAP <65) despite adequate fluid bolus (≥30ml/kg crystalloid) — needs vasopressors (noradrenaline) within 1 hour, not more fluid.
- **Meningococcal sepsis / purpura fulminans**
  - Single finding: non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash with fever and hypotension — give IV ceftriaxone 2g (or cefotaxime) immediately; do not wait for LP or imaging.
- **Also consider:** massive pulmonary embolism (distended neck veins, acute right heart strain) or anaphylaxis (wheals, stridor, rapid onset) — both can mimic septic shock but demand different treatments.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** fever/shivering, confusion or reduced consciousness, tachypnoea, tachycardia, hypotension, oliguria, mottled skin, and a history of infection (chest, urine, abdomen, skin, line).
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Temperature >38°C or <36°C
  - HR >90, RR >20, SBP <100 or drop >40mmHg from baseline
  - Capillary refill >2s, peripheral vasoconstriction, altered mental state (AVPU less than 'A')
  - Local signs: crackles, suprapubic tenderness, costovertebral angle pain, peritonism, cellulitis, surgical wound erythema.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Sepsis** → infection with organ dysfunction (elevated lactate, hypotension, reduced GCS)
- **SIRS from non-infectious causes** (pancreatitis, burns, trauma, autoimmune flare) → no clear infection source, negative cultures, non-response to antibiotics
- **Anaphylaxis** → acute onset of urticaria, wheeze, stridor, hypotension after allergen exposure
- **Pulmonary embolism** → acute dyspnoea, pleuritic pain, haemoptysis, raised D-dimer, RV strain on ECG
- **Cardiogenic shock** → crackles, raised JVP, cool peripheries, sign of fluid overload
- **Hypovolaemic shock** → history of bleeding/diarrhoea, low JVP, high haematocrit
- **Adrenal crisis** → hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hyperpigmentation, poor response to fluids
- **Splitter:** Check lactate and look for a clear infection source (e.g., chest X-ray infiltrates, positive urinalysis) — if absent and rash present → consider anaphylaxis or meningitis first.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - NEWS2 (chart) – score ≥5 indicates sepsis trigger
  - Lactate (venous or arterial) – >2 mmol/L suggests tissue hypoperfusion; >4 mmol/L = high risk
  - Blood cultures (two sets) – before antibiotics if possible (but do not delay antibiotics >1 hour)
  - Urinalysis + urine culture
  - ECG (to rule out STEMI/arrhythmia if hypotensive)
  - Capillary refill, skin temperature, respiratory rate
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, creatinine, LFTs, coagulation screen, CRP, procalcitonin (if available)
  - Blood gas (ABG/VBG) – pH, lactate, glucose, base excess
  - Consider: group/crossmatch if suspected bleeding; troponin if chest pain/ECG changes
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Chest X-ray (for pneumonia) – erect or supine if unable to sit
  - **Second-line/if unclear source:** CT scan (chest/abdomen/pelvis) with IV contrast; US for biliary/renal obstruction; consider echocardiography if endocarditis or heart failure.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **qSOFA** (quick Sequential Organ Failure Assessment): ≥2 of altered mental state (GCS <15), RR ≥22, SBP ≤100 – predicts poor outcome in non-ICU setting.
  - **NEWS2** (National Early Warning Score 2): trigger for escalation at ≥5 (or ≥3 in any single parameter).
  - **Lactate clearance:** repeat lactate after 2-4 hours; failure to drop by ≥20% suggests inadequate resuscitation.
- **Never-miss test:** **Lactate** in any patient presenting with suspected infection or hypotension — omission can miss occult hypoperfusion and delay aggressive fluid resuscitation.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Follow ABCDE: give high-flow oxygen if SpO2 <92% (or <88% in CO2 retainers with COPD), secure IV access x 2 large bore.
  - **Fluid resuscitation:** IV crystalloid (e.g., Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline) – give 500ml bolus over <15 min; reassess. Repeat up to 30ml/kg in first hour if hypotensive or lactate >2. Use dynamic assessment (passive leg raise, IVC collapsibility) in frail/fluid-sensitive patients.
  - **Antibiotics:** Give broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within **1 hour** of recognition per local antimicrobial policy (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV, or ceftriaxone 2g IV + metronidazole 500mg IV; for neutropenic sepsis: piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem). *Do not delay for cultures.*
  - **Source control:** remove infected lines and drains, drain abscesses (surgical/radiology), decompress obstructed biliary/urinary tract (ERCP/JP drain).
  - **Vasopressors:** If BP remains low after 30ml/kg fluid – start noradrenaline via central line (or peripheral if not possible) titrated to MAP ≥65.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue IV fluids guided by dynamic assessment, lactate clearance, urine output (target >0.5ml/kg/hr), and clinical perfusion.
  - Review antibiotic spectrum at 48-72h: de-escalate based on cultures and sensitivities.
  - Monitor NEWS2 hourly. Repeat lactate at 2-4h; then 6-12h if improving.
  - Assess for occult source: line sites, pressure ulcers, sinuses, surgical wounds.
  - Involve critical care outreach / ICU early for escalating organ support (inotropes, NIV, RRT).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Ensure completion of antibiotic course (typically 7-10 days, tailored to source).
  - Arrange follow-up in sepsis clinic or GP for post-sepsis syndrome (fatigue, cognitive impairment, depression).
  - Check for residual organ failure (renal, respiratory) and arrange appropriate referrals.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: crystalloid (Hartmann’s or 0.9% saline) – e.g., 1L over 8h if stable, adjust per assessment.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC once daily (check weight, renal function).
  - Diet: NBM until source control achieved or safe swallow confirmed (if intubated or post-operative).
  - Observation frequency: NEWS2 – at least hourly for first 6 hours, then 4-hourly if stable.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (empiric broad-spectrum):**
  - *Community-acquired pneumonia:* co-amoxiclav 1.2g IV TDS + clarithromycin 500mg IV BD (or doxycycline 200mg IV/PO if allergic)
  - *UTI / intra-abdominal:* piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV TDS (or ceftriaxone 2g IV OD + metronidazole 500mg IV TDS)
  - *Meningitis:* dexamethasone 10mg IV (before antibiotics) + ceftriaxone 2g IV BD + amoxicillin 2g IV 4-hourly (if Listeria risk)
  - *Neutropenic sepsis:* piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV QDS (or meropenem 1g IV TDS)
  - *Vasopressor:* noradrenaline 0.05-0.5 mcg/kg/min (titrate; start at 5-10 mcg/min in 50ml syringe pump)
- **Common alternatives:** Meropenem (if ESBL suspected); vancomycin (for MRSA risk) – add if line sepsis or known colonisation; gentamicin (single dose 5-7mg/kg IV) for synergistic effect – **must check levels before second dose**.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Co-amoxiclav: avoid in penicillin allergy (anaphylaxis); caution in cholestatic jaundice.
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam: similar penicillin cross-reactivity; can cause neutropenia with prolonged use.
  - Ceftriaxone: avoid in neonates <41 weeks (displaces bilirubin); can precipitate gallstones.
  - Vancomycin: requires trough level monitoring (target 15-20 mg/L); give slowly over ≥1h to avoid red man syndrome.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** [check local policy] – e.g., vancomycin, gentamicin, piperacillin-tazobactam doses reduced in renal impairment; ceftriaxone mostly safe (though avoid in severe renal + hepatic failure). For meropenem, reduce frequency in CrCl <50 ml/min.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Not giving antibiotics within 1 hour** – each hour delay increases mortality. Also **giving IV vancomycin too fast** (red man syndrome) or **checking gentamicin levels before the second dose** (toxic accumulative).

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Septic shock (refractory hypotension), acute kidney injury, ARDS, DIC (disseminated intravascular coagulation), metabolic acidosis, multiorgan failure, death.
- **Chronic:**
  - Post-sepsis syndrome (cognitive impairment, fatigue, PTSD), limb amputation (if purpura fulminans), chronic kidney disease, critical illness polyneuropathy.

### Prognosis
- Mortality from septic shock is 30-50% if not recognised early; with timely antibiotics and resuscitation can drop to 20-30%. Recovery often prolonged – months of rehabilitation for critical illness survivors.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check the source** – examine chest, urine, skin, lines, surgical wounds; take cultures before antibiotics but never delay antibiotics >1 hour.
- **Repeat lactate at 2-4 hours** – failure to drop by ≥20% means inadequate resuscitation or ongoing infection; escalate to senior for source imaging or vasopressors.
- **Document NEWS2 and sepsis care bundle** – ensures audit trail and triggers early escalation by nursing staff.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Over-resuscitating with fluids** in frail/elderly patients with known heart failure – causes pulmonary oedema. Use dynamic assessment (passive leg raise) or early vasopressors.
- **Waiting for lab results before giving antibiotics** – treat empirically; de-escalate later.
- **Assuming “warm sepsis” (wide pulse pressure, bounding pulses) is benign** – still has high lactate and organ dysfunction; needs the same urgent management.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** any new confusion, breathing difficulty, leg swelling/pain, rash, recurrence of fever, inability to tolerate oral fluids/antibiotics, or urine output <0.5ml/kg/h for >6 hours.
- **Follow-up:** GP review within 48 hours (if discharged from ED) or sepsis outpatient clinic within 1-2 weeks for post-sepsis syndrome assessment.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - MAP <65 despite 30ml/kg fluid (consider noradrenaline)
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L or persistent >2 mmol/L after 2-4 hours
  - NEWS2 ≥7 (or increasing by ≥3 despite treatment)
  - GCS <13 or acute change
  - Urine output <0.5ml/kg/h for 2 consecutive hours
  - New oxygen requirement >40% FiO2 or SpO2 <92%
- **Crash team if:** PEA arrest, asystole, unresponsive, no breathing.
